refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of what 'availability' means (uptime ratio), the measurement period, and inclusion of safety-driven constraints. High safety criticality means unavailability due to safety interventions must be distinguished from failure-driven unavailability in root cause analysis.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, safety-hold, failed) with timestamped records; post-evaluation statistical analysis of uptime ratio against the 0.97 threshold; traceability to failure modes and safety events.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all state transitions (operational, maintenance, safety-hold, failure) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault tolerance mechanisms.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall ensure safety-driven shutdowns do not degrade measured availability below the 0.97 target when properly scheduled.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and reporting system that aggregates telemetry from all subsystems, calculates uptime metrics in real-time, and triggers predictive maintenance alerts based on degradation thresholds.

DP-B: Distribute availability monitoring across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules with local state tracking and periodic synchronization to a central dashboard; rely on operator awareness and manual reporting.
DP-C: Implement redundant subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual safety circuits) to mask failures and maintain operational availability without explicit monitoring.

DP-D: Combine centralized telemetry-driven monitoring (DP-A) with redundancy in critical subsystems (DP-C) and predictive maintenance scheduling to achieve 0.97 availability through both fault tolerance and proactive intervention.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining (1) centralized availability monitoring via continuous telemetry aggregation with real-time uptime calculation and predictive maintenance alerts, (2) redundancy in safety-critical subsystems (dual control channels, fault-tolerant power distribution), and (3) scheduled predictive maintenance based on degradation thresholds and historical failure patterns. This DP balances fault tolerance (reducing unplanned downtime) with visibility (enabling proactive intervention) and safety compliance (ensuring safety-driven shutdowns are logged and distinguished from failures).

potential_sources: DP-D couples availability monitoring (telemetry aggregation) with Vehicle subsystem health (sensor data, state transitions) and Teleoperation subsystem status (network link quality, operator availability). These couplings are acceptable because they are information flows (Telemetry interface) rather than functional dependencies; the monitoring system does not dictate how Vehicle or Teleoperation operate, only observes and reports.

DP-D couples predictive maintenance scheduling with Maintenance/Quality stakeholder workflow; this is a process coupling that requires clear handoff protocols but does not create circular functional dependencies.

Redundancy in safety-critical subsystems (part of DP-D) couples with Safety Services module; this is a required coupling to ensure safety integrity and is explicitly managed via SafetyAlert interface.
coupling_check.notes: No circular or hidden couplings detected. The DP respects the FR-DP independence axiom by solving the availability problem through monitoring and redundancy without imposing new functional requirements on Vehicle or Teleoperation.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Telemetry data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems is reliable and available with sufficient granularity to detect state transitions and degradation trends.
ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance models (thresholds, degradation curves) are validated against historical failure data and are accurate enough to prevent unplanned downtime.
ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems (dual control channels, etc.) are designed and tested to meet safety integrity levels (SIL) required by the worksite safety plan.
RISK: If telemetry is delayed or lossy, availability calculations may be inaccurate, leading to false confidence or missed maintenance windows.
RISK: If predictive maintenance is triggered too aggressively, scheduled downtime may exceed the 0.97 target; if too conservatively, unplanned failures may occur.
RISK: Redundancy adds cost, weight, and complexity; customer may not accept the trade-off if availability target can be met with simpler design.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with customer the acceptable ratio of planned maintenance downtime to total scheduled time; define the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year) and whether it includes ramp-up/commissioning phases. Specify the required accuracy of telemetry (latency, loss rate) and the confidence level for predictive maintenance models (e.g., 95% confidence that predicted failures occur within ±10% of predicted time window).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that owns the availability KPI and coordinates monitoring, redundancy, and maintenance scheduling across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It includes the centralized availability monitoring system, predictive maintenance logic, and the integration of redundant subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Continuous telemetry streams from Vehicle (motor health, sensor readings, state transitions) and Teleoperation (network link quality, operator status, control latency) feed into the centralized monitoring system for availability calculation and predictive maintenance triggering.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Availability monitoring system sends maintenance scheduling tasks and predictive maintenance alerts to Maintenance/Quality team via Tasking interface; also sends operational directives (e.g., reduce load, enter safe mode) to Vehicle and Teleoperation based on degradation thresholds.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives SafetyAlert signals from Safety Services (e.g., emergency stop, safety-hold) and logs them as state transitions; sends availability status and redundancy health to Safety Services to inform safety decision-making.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Availability monitoring system monitors Network Link quality (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) as a key availability metric; degradation in Network Link directly impacts Teleoperation availability and is factored into overall system availability calculation.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the availability KPI because it is the top-level system integration boundary. The centralized monitoring system, predictive maintenance logic, and redundancy coordination are all system-level concerns that must be owned at this level. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry aggregation and alert generation are contained; actual subsystem health data and control logic remain in Vehicle and Teleoperation.

Vehicle could own local availability monitoring for its own subsystems (motor, sensors, power distribution), but the system-level availability KPI and predictive maintenance scheduling must be at NGHE Solution level. If Vehicle owned the full DP, availability logic would be diffused across Vehicle and Teleoperation, making it difficult to coordinate redundancy and maintenance scheduling. Not recommended as primary owner.

Teleoperation could own monitoring of its own subsystems (network link, operator interface, control latency), but the system-level availability KPI and integration with Vehicle redundancy must be at NGHE Solution level. High diffusion risk if Teleoperation owned the full DP. Not recommended as primary owner.

Safety Services is responsible for safety-critical decision-making and emergency response, not for availability monitoring or predictive maintenance. While Safety Services must receive availability status and redundancy health information, it should not own the availability KPI itself. High diffusion risk and misalignment with Safety Services' core responsibility. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution owns the availability KPI and the centralized monitoring/predictive maintenance system. Vehicle and Teleoperation own their respective subsystem health monitoring and provide telemetry via the Telemetry interface. Safety Services receives availability status via SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces but does not own the KPI. This containment minimizes diffusion and aligns ownership with system-level integration responsibility.
containment_risks: RISK: If telemetry from Vehicle or Teleoperation is incomplete or delayed, NGHE Solution's availability calculation may be inaccurate. Mitigation: Define telemetry requirements (granularity, latency, loss tolerance) in interface specifications and validate during integration testing.

RISK: Predictive maintenance logic in NGHE Solution may conflict with Vehicle or Teleoperation operational constraints (e.g., maintenance window not feasible during critical mission phase). Mitigation: Implement a maintenance scheduling negotiation protocol where NGHE Solution proposes maintenance windows and Vehicle/Teleoperation confirm feasibility.

RISK: Redundancy in safety-critical subsystems (part of DP-D) may require changes to Vehicle or Safety Services design. Mitigation: Ensure redundancy requirements are flowed down to Vehicle and Safety Services early in design; coordinate via SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces.

RISK: NGHE Solution module may become a bottleneck if it must process high-volume telemetry and make real-time maintenance decisions. Mitigation: Design NGHE Solution with scalable architecture (e.g., distributed monitoring agents, asynchronous event processing).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish clear interface contracts for Telemetry (data schema, update frequency, required fields), Tasking (maintenance task format, priority levels, acknowledgment protocol), and SafetyAlert (alert types, severity levels, response time requirements). Define the predictive maintenance algorithm and its inputs/outputs explicitly. Specify the redundancy architecture (which subsystems are redundant, how failover is triggered, how redundancy health is monitored).

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (hybrid monitoring + redundancy + predictive maintenance) directly addresses the FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific technologies) and is decomposable into sub-DPs for monitoring, redundancy, and maintenance scheduling. The DP respects the independence axiom by solving availability through observation and proactive intervention, not by imposing new functional requirements on Vehicle or Teleoperation.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry aggregation logic is centralized in NGHE Solution, but Vehicle and Teleoperation must provide telemetry data. This is a required coupling but is managed via the Telemetry interface; no diffusion of core availability logic.

Predictive maintenance scheduling is centralized in NGHE Solution, but Maintenance/Quality team must execute maintenance tasks. This is a process coupling managed via Tasking interface; no diffusion of availability logic.

Redundancy in safety-critical subsystems may require design changes in Vehicle and Safety Services. This is a design coupling that must be managed via early requirements flow-down and interface coordination.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the clear owner of the availability KPI and centralized monitoring/predictive maintenance system. Vehicle and Teleoperation are data providers (via Telemetry) and task executors (via Tasking), not owners of availability logic. Safety Services is an observer and decision-maker (via SafetyAlert), not an owner. Containment is strong; diffusion is minimal and well-managed via interfaces.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability can be verified by continuous logging of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, safety-hold, failure) and post-evaluation statistical analysis of uptime ratio. Telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation provides the data needed for verification. Predictive maintenance effectiveness can be verified by comparing predicted failure times to actual failure times. Redundancy can be verified by fault injection testing and operational data analysis.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The FR is clearly allocated to NGHE Solution as the owner of the availability KPI. Sub-FRs are allocated to Vehicle (FR-NGHE-004.1, FR-NGHE-004.2 for subsystem health), Teleoperation (FR-NGHE-004.1, FR-NGHE-004.3 for network link and operator status), and Safety Services (FR-NGHE-004.4 for safety-driven shutdowns). The allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Define the evaluation period for availability measurement (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year) and clarify whether it includes ramp-up/commissioning phases or only steady-state operation.
Specify the required accuracy of telemetry (latency, loss rate, granularity) and the confidence level for predictive maintenance models.
Establish interface contracts for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link with explicit data schemas, update frequencies, and response time requirements.
Define the redundancy architecture (which subsystems are redundant, failover triggers, redundancy health monitoring) and flow down redundancy requirements to Vehicle and Safety Services.
Clarify the acceptable ratio of planned maintenance downtime to total scheduled time and how it is factored into the 0.97 availability target.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs addressing monitoring, redundancy, and predictive maintenance. The refined FR and selected DP are sound and pass the gate review, but they must be decomposed further to enable detailed design and implementation. The sub-FRs identified (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) are the natural next level of decomposition and should be refined and assigned to specific modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, NGHE Solution) in the next iteration.
decision.justification_detail: The availability KPI is a composite measure that depends on multiple subsystems and processes. Decomposing it into sub-FRs allows each subsystem to own specific aspects of availability (e.g., Vehicle owns subsystem health monitoring, Teleoperation owns network link quality, Safety Services owns safety-driven shutdown logging). This decomposition also enables clearer verification strategies and allocation of design responsibility.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all state transitions (operational, maintenance, safety-hold, failure) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault tolerance mechanisms.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall ensure safety-driven shutdowns do not degrade measured availability below the 0.97 target when properly scheduled.

assumptions: Telemetry data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems is reliable and available with sufficient granularity to detect state transitions and degradation trends.
Predictive maintenance models are validated against historical failure data and are accurate enough to prevent unplanned downtime.
Redundant subsystems are designed and tested to meet safety integrity levels required by the worksite safety plan.
The evaluation period for availability measurement is defined and agreed with the customer (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year).
Planned maintenance downtime is acceptable and does not degrade the 0.97 availability target when properly scheduled.
Safety-driven shutdowns are logged and distinguished from failure-driven unavailability in root cause analysis.
open_risks: If telemetry is delayed or lossy, availability calculations may be inaccurate, leading to false confidence or missed maintenance windows.
If predictive maintenance is triggered too aggressively, scheduled downtime may exceed the 0.97 target; if too conservatively, unplanned failures may occur.
Redundancy adds cost, weight, and complexity; customer may not accept the trade-off if availability target can be met with simpler design.
NGHE Solution module may become a bottleneck if it must process high-volume telemetry and make real-time maintenance decisions.
Redundancy in safety-critical subsystems may require design changes to Vehicle or Safety Services that are not yet fully understood.
evidence_needed: Historical failure data from similar equipment to validate predictive maintenance models.
Telemetry requirements specification (granularity, latency, loss tolerance) agreed with Vehicle and Teleoperation teams.
Safety integrity level (SIL) requirements for redundant subsystems from Safety Services and worksite safety plan.
Customer agreement on evaluation period, acceptable planned maintenance downtime, and confidence level for predictive maintenance.
Redundancy architecture design and failover testing results to validate fault tolerance.
Integration test results showing accurate availability calculation and predictive maintenance triggering.
